title: Today's Tech Brief: Dashlane explains how attackers managed to download encrypted password vaults
summary: A brief look at tech headlines, including a Dashlane security issue and a marketing demanded IT add website feature
imageAlt: Image from source article for Dashlane explains how attackers managed to download encrypted password vaults
imageCaption: Dashlane explains how attackers managed to download encrypted password vaults
imageSourceTitle: Dashlane explains how attackers managed to download encrypted password vaults

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's Tech Brief. Let's dive into some fresh tech news. First up, Dashlane had a bit of a security scare. Apparently, attackers managed to download encrypted password vaults from their system. Now, I know what you're thinking - isn't encryption supposed to keep data safe? Well, here's what happened: Dashlane said attackers mounted a coordinated hacking campaign against them, and somehow, they got away with encrypted vaults. The good news is that Dashlane says the attackers didn't get the master decryption key, so the data is still pretty safe. This incident just goes to show that even with top-notch security measures, there's always room for improvement.

In a story that's equal parts baffling and amusing, marketing demanded IT add a website feature that was already working. Yes, you read that right. According to a report, the marketing team asked the IT department to implement a feature, only to find out it was already live and working just fine. I can imagine the conversation: 'Can we add this feature?' 'Uh, it's already there.' 'Oh, great, can you make it work then?' It's a funny example of how miscommunication can happen, even in the tech world.

Dashlane explains how attackers managed to download encrypted password vaults is also worth a quick mention.
